questions.text: The aging process of the human body is slowed by motion at:
questions.subject: Physics

options.explanation: Moving at very slow speeds does not have a significant impact on time dilation or the aging process according to the theory of relativity. At slow speeds, relativistic effects are negligible.

While very high speeds near the speed of light can lead to time dilation, the speeds achievable by humans in everyday activities are far from the speeds where relativistic effects become significant. Thus, high speeds encountered in normal activities do not significantly affect the aging process due to time dilation.

options.text: Very high speed along a circular path
options.explanation: This is based on the concept of time dilation from Einstein's theory of relativity. When an object moves at a very high speed, especially near the speed of light, time appears to pass more slowly for that object relative to a stationary observer.
This option is incorrect because, as mentioned earlier, motion at high speeds (especially near the speed of light) can have an impact on time dilation and, in theory, could affect the aging process.

questions.explanation: This is based on the concept of time dilation from Einstein's theory of relativity. When an object moves at a very high speed, especially near the speed of light, time appears to pass more slowly for that object relative to a stationary observer.

questions.text: The amount of energy needed to remove electrons from the metal surface depends upon:
questions.subject: Physics

options.explanation: Work is done when a force is applied to an object, and the object moves in the direction of the force.

The energy required to remove an electron from the surface of a metal is known as the work function. It represents the minimum amount of energy needed to overcome the attractive forces between the electron and the metal surface. The formula is given by:ϕ=h⋅fϕ is the work function,h is Planck's constant (6.626×10−34 J⋅s)f is the frequency of the incident light.
Power is the rate at which work is done or the rate at which energy is transferred or transformed.
Wavelength is a property of waves, representing the distance between successive peaks (or troughs) of a wave.

questions.explanation: The energy required to remove an electron from the surface of a metal is known as the work function. It represents the minimum amount of energy needed to overcome the attractive forces between the electron and the metal surface. The formula is given by:ϕ=h⋅fϕ is the work function,h is Planck's constant (6.626×10−34 J⋅s)f is the frequency of the incident light.

questions.text: A photocell with a constant potential difference of V volt across it is illuminated by a point source from a distance of 25 cm. When the source is moved to a distance of 1m. The electrons are emitted by the photocell:
questions.subject: Physics

options.explanation: The energy of electrons emitted by the photocell is proportional to the intensity of light. If the intensity decreases by a factor of 1/16, the energy of the electrons will also decrease by the same factor.

I2/I1= (r1/r2)2As r1= 0.25mAnd r2= 1m we have I2/I1=1/16This is correct based on the change in intensity. If the intensity decreases by a factor of 1/16, the number of electrons emitted will decrease by the same factor.
This option is not correct due to the explanation stated above.
The momentum of the electrons is related to their energy. If the energy decreases by a factor of 1/16, the momentum will decrease by the same factor.

questions.explanation: I2/I1= (r1/r2)2As r1= 0.25mAnd r2= 1m we have I2/I1=1/16This is correct based on the change in intensity. If the intensity decreases by a factor of 1/16, the number of electrons emitted will decrease by the same factor.

questions.text: Light of a certain wavelength and intensity ejects photoelectrons from a metal plate. Then this beam is replaced by another beam of smaller wavelength and smaller intensity. As a result:
questions.subject: Physics

options.explanation: This is unlikely because changing the wavelength and intensity of light can affect the energy of the photons and, consequently, the photoelectric effect.
If the new light has a smaller wavelength and smaller intensity, it might not have enough energy to overcome the work function, and the photoelectric effect could stop.

options.text: K.E of the photoelectrons decreases but the strength of the photoelectric current increases
options.explanation: This contradicts the basic principles of the photoelectric effect. The kinetic energy of the ejected photoelectrons depends on the energy of the incident photons. If the wavelength is smaller, the energy is higher.

options.text: K.E of the photoelectrons decreases but the strength of the photoelectric current decreases
options.explanation: This is a plausible scenario. If the new light has a smaller wavelength (higher energy) and smaller intensity, the kinetic energy of the ejected photoelectrons may decrease, but the overall current might also decrease due to the reduced number of photoelectrons. The photoelectric effect can be described using the following formula:E=hc/λ−ϕwhere E is the kinetic energy of the emitted photoelectronsh is Planck's constant (6.626×10−34J⋅s),c is the speed of lightλ is the wavelengthϕ is the work function of the material (the minimum energy required to remove an electron from the surface).

questions.explanation: This is a plausible scenario. If the new light has a smaller wavelength (higher energy) and smaller intensity, the kinetic energy of the ejected photoelectrons may decrease, but the overall current might also decrease due to the reduced number of photoelectrons. The photoelectric effect can be described using the following formula:E=hc/λ−ϕwhere E is the kinetic energy of the emitted photoelectronsh is Planck's constant (6.626×10−34J⋅s),c is the speed of lightλ is the wavelengthϕ is the work function of the material (the minimum energy required to remove an electron from the surface).

questions.text: Two inputs of NAND gates are shorted. This gate is equivalent to:
questions.subject: Physics

options.explanation: Due to the explanation above, this option is incorrect.
Due to the explanation above, this option is incorrect.

The NAND gate is a digital logic gate that operates the AND gate followed by the NOT gate. When both inputs of a NAND gate are shorted together, it effectively becomes a gate with a single input (the connected inputs). The output of a NAND gate with one input is the logical inversion (NOT) of that input.
Due to the explanation above, this option is incorrect.

questions.explanation: The NAND gate is a digital logic gate that operates the AND gate followed by the NOT gate. When both inputs of a NAND gate are shorted together, it effectively becomes a gate with a single input (the connected inputs). The output of a NAND gate with one input is the logical inversion (NOT) of that input.

questions.text: The de Broglie wave length of an electron moving with a velocity of 1.5 x 108 m/s is equal to that of photon. the ratio of K.E of the electron to that of photon (C = 3 x108 m/s) is

questions.text: The maximum K.E of emitted photoelectrons depend on
questions.subject: Physics

options.explanation: Intensity of the incident light: While the intensity of light can affect the number of photoelectrons emitted, it does not affect the maximum kinetic energy (KE) of each emitted electron.Frequency of the incident light: According to Einstein's photoelectric effect equation, the maximum kinetic energy (KE_max) of emitted photoelectrons is directly proportional to the frequency (f) of the incident light:KE_max = h * f - W_0where:h is Planck's constantW_0 is the work function of the material (minimum energy required to eject an electron)Therefore, increasing the frequency of the incident light increases the maximum kinetic energy of the emitted photoelectrons.Temperature of the photosensitive surface: The temperature of the photosensitive surface does not influence the maximum kinetic energy of emitted photoelectrons as long as the temperature is low enough to avoid significant thermal emission (electrons being ejected due to thermal energy instead of light).Therefore, the frequency of the incident light is the primary factor determining the maximum kinetic energy of emitted photoelectrons

questions.explanation: Intensity of the incident light: While the intensity of light can affect the number of photoelectrons emitted, it does not affect the maximum kinetic energy (KE) of each emitted electron.Frequency of the incident light: According to Einstein's photoelectric effect equation, the maximum kinetic energy (KE_max) of emitted photoelectrons is directly proportional to the frequency (f) of the incident light:KE_max = h * f - W_0where:h is Planck's constantW_0 is the work function of the material (minimum energy required to eject an electron)Therefore, increasing the frequency of the incident light increases the maximum kinetic energy of the emitted photoelectrons.Temperature of the photosensitive surface: The temperature of the photosensitive surface does not influence the maximum kinetic energy of emitted photoelectrons as long as the temperature is low enough to avoid significant thermal emission (electrons being ejected due to thermal energy instead of light).Therefore, the frequency of the incident light is the primary factor determining the maximum kinetic energy of emitted photoelectrons

questions.text: Power radiated by black body at temperature T1 is P and it radiates maximum energy at a wavelength 11. If the temperature of the black body is changed from T1 to T2, it radiates max energy at a wavelength of 11/2. The power radiated at T2 is
questions.subject: Physics

options.explanation: This value is too low because the power increases significantly with temperature according to the Stefan-Boltzmann law
This value is also an underestimate of the actual increase in power with increasing temperature
This value falls between the (4P) and option c (8P) but is still inaccurate based on the relationship between power and temperature for black bodies

The information provided in the image states that:The power radiated by a black body at temperature T₁ is P, and it radiates maximum energy at a wavelength λ₁.When the temperature of the black body changes from T₁ to T₂, it starts radiating maximum energy at a wavelength of λ₁/2.We are asked to find the power radiated at temperature T₂.Key concept:Wein's displacement law states that the wavelength (λ) at which a black body radiates the maximum intensity is inversely proportional to its temperature (T). Mathematically, it can be expressed as:λT = constantwhere the constant is approximately 2.897 x 10^-3 m⋅K.Reasoning:Relating λ₁ and T₁ using Wein's displacement law:λ₁T₁ = constantRelating λ₂ and T₂ using Wein's displacement law:λ₂T₂ = constantWe are given:λ₂ = λ₁/2Substituting the second equation into the first equation:λ₁T₁ = (λ₁/2)T₂Solving for T₂:T₂ = 2T₁Relation between power radiated (P) and temperature (T) for a black body:P ∝ T⁴ (Stefan-Boltzmann law)Taking the ratio of the power radiated at T₂ (P₂) to the power radiated at T₁ (P₁):P₂/P₁ = (T₂⁴ / T₁⁴)Substituting T₂ = 2T₁ from step 5:P₂/P₁ = (2T₁⁴ / T₁⁴) = 2⁴ = 16Therefore, the power radiated at temperature T₂ (P₂) is 16 times the power radiated at temperature T₁ (P₁). This translates to 16P as the answer.

questions.explanation: The information provided in the image states that:The power radiated by a black body at temperature T₁ is P, and it radiates maximum energy at a wavelength λ₁.When the temperature of the black body changes from T₁ to T₂, it starts radiating maximum energy at a wavelength of λ₁/2.We are asked to find the power radiated at temperature T₂.Key concept:Wein's displacement law states that the wavelength (λ) at which a black body radiates the maximum intensity is inversely proportional to its temperature (T). Mathematically, it can be expressed as:λT = constantwhere the constant is approximately 2.897 x 10^-3 m⋅K.Reasoning:Relating λ₁ and T₁ using Wein's displacement law:λ₁T₁ = constantRelating λ₂ and T₂ using Wein's displacement law:λ₂T₂ = constantWe are given:λ₂ = λ₁/2Substituting the second equation into the first equation:λ₁T₁ = (λ₁/2)T₂Solving for T₂:T₂ = 2T₁Relation between power radiated (P) and temperature (T) for a black body:P ∝ T⁴ (Stefan-Boltzmann law)Taking the ratio of the power radiated at T₂ (P₂) to the power radiated at T₁ (P₁):P₂/P₁ = (T₂⁴ / T₁⁴)Substituting T₂ = 2T₁ from step 5:P₂/P₁ = (2T₁⁴ / T₁⁴) = 2⁴ = 16Therefore, the power radiated at temperature T₂ (P₂) is 16 times the power radiated at temperature T₁ (P₁). This translates to 16P as the answer.

questions.text: if we consider electrons and photons of the same wavelength, then they will have the same
questions.subject: Physics

options.explanation: Electrons and photons both exhibit wave-particle duality as per quantum mechanics.For a wave of same wavelength λ, the frequency f of the wave will be same for both electrons and photons based on the wave equation:λ = v/fFrom Planck's equation, we know the energy E of a photon is related to its frequency by:E = hfFor electrons, the de Broglie wavelength λ is related to its momentum p by:λ = h/pIf electrons and photons have the same wavelength λ, then they must have:Same frequency fSame energy ESame momentum pTherefore, if electrons and photons have the same wavelength, the physical quantity they will have in common is momentum (p).

questions.explanation: Electrons and photons both exhibit wave-particle duality as per quantum mechanics.For a wave of same wavelength λ, the frequency f of the wave will be same for both electrons and photons based on the wave equation:λ = v/fFrom Planck's equation, we know the energy E of a photon is related to its frequency by:E = hfFor electrons, the de Broglie wavelength λ is related to its momentum p by:λ = h/pIf electrons and photons have the same wavelength λ, then they must have:Same frequency fSame energy ESame momentum pTherefore, if electrons and photons have the same wavelength, the physical quantity they will have in common is momentum (p).

questions.text: According to the postulates of the theory of relativity, a fourth dimension has been added to the three dimensions already associated with a Cartesian frame of reference. Which is the fourth dimension?
questions.subject: Physics

options.explanation: Space itself is one of the three dimensions (x, y, and z) in a Cartesian frame. It's not considered the fourth dimension; time is added as the fourth dimension in the theory of relativity.

An inertial frame of reference is a frame of reference in which an object either is at rest or moves at a constant velocity. It is not considered the fourth dimension; rather, it's a concept related to the observer's state of motion.
The speed of light (c) is a fundamental constant in the theory of relativity. While it's crucial to the theory, it is not considered the fourth dimension.
According to the theory of relativity, specifically Einstein's theory of special relativity, the fourth dimension added to the three dimensions of space is time.

questions.explanation: According to the theory of relativity, specifically Einstein's theory of special relativity, the fourth dimension added to the three dimensions of space is time.

questions.text: The wave nature of an electron is illustrated by its
questions.subject: Physics

options.explanation: The photoelectric effect demonstrates the particle-like behavior of light, not the wave nature of electrons.
The Compton effect demonstrates the particle-like behavior of photons, not the wave nature of electrons.
The penetrating effect does not specifically illustrate the wave nature of electrons.

Diffraction is a phenomenon observed when electrons are passed through a narrow slit, demonstrating their wave-like behavior as they interfere with each other. This supports the wave-particle duality of electrons.

questions.explanation: Diffraction is a phenomenon observed when electrons are passed through a narrow slit, demonstrating their wave-like behavior as they interfere with each other. This supports the wave-particle duality of electrons.

questions.text: The combination of AND and NOT gate is called
questions.subject: Physics

options.explanation: A NAND gate is a combination of an AND gate followed by a NOT gate. It produces a high output (logic 1) only when both of its inputs are low (logic 0).
A NOR gate is a combination of OR gate followed by a NOT gate. It produces a low output (logic 0) only when both of its inputs are high (logic 1).
An OR gate is a logical gate that performs logical disjunction. It outputs a high signal (logic 1) if at least one of its inputs is high (logic 1).
An XOR gate is a logical gate that outputs a high signal (logic 1) if the number of high inputs is odd. It is not a combination of AND and NOT gates.

questions.explanation: A NAND gate is a combination of an AND gate followed by a NOT gate. It produces a high output (logic 1) only when both of its inputs are low (logic 0).

questions.text: If the temperature of the black body becomes double, the intensity of radiation from it will become
questions.subject: Physics

options.explanation: According to Stefan-Boltzmann law, the intensity of radiation from a black body is directly proportional to the fourth power of its absolute temperature (in Kelvin). Therefore, if the temperature doubles, the intensity of radiation will increase by a factor of \(2^4 = 16\), not double.

According to Stefan-Boltzmann law, the intensity of radiation from a black body is directly proportional to the fourth power of its absolute temperature (in Kelvin). Therefore, if the temperature doubles, the intensity of radiation will increase by a factor of \(2^4 = 16\).
The correct increase in intensity of radiation when the temperature doubles is \(2^4 = 16\), not six times.
Sixteen times is the correct increase in intensity of radiation when the temperature doubles according to Stefan-Boltzmann law.

questions.explanation: According to Stefan-Boltzmann law, the intensity of radiation from a black body is directly proportional to the fourth power of its absolute temperature (in Kelvin). Therefore, if the temperature doubles, the intensity of radiation will increase by a factor of \(2^4 = 16\).

questions.text: The scattering angle for which the Compton shift in wavelength is equal to the Compton wavelength is
questions.subject: Physics

options.explanation: When the change in wavelength equals the Compton wavelength, the scattering angle is 90 degrees.\[ \lambda' - \lambda = \frac{h}{m_e c}(1 - \cos(\theta)) \]\[ \cos(\theta) = 1 \Rightarrow \theta = 90^\circ \]
At 0 degrees, the change in wavelength is not equal to the Compton wavelength.\[ \cos(\theta) = 1 \]
At 45 degrees, the change in wavelength is not equal to the Compton wavelength.\[ \cos(\theta) = \frac{1}{\sqrt{2}} \]
At 180 degrees, the change in wavelength is not equal to the Compton wavelength.\[ \cos(\theta) = -1 \]

questions.explanation: When the change in wavelength equals the Compton wavelength, the scattering angle is 90 degrees.\[ \lambda' - \lambda = \frac{h}{m_e c}(1 - \cos(\theta)) \]\[ \cos(\theta) = 1 \Rightarrow \theta = 90^\circ \]

questions.text: The absolute standard of rest is
questions.subject: Physics

options.explanation: According to the principles of special relativity, there is no absolute standard of rest. This means that there is no single reference frame that can be considered to be at rest. The concept of rest is relative, depending on the observer's frame of reference.

questions.text: Which statement is universally correct?
questions.subject: Physics

options.text: Motion can alter time but not space
Motion can alter time and space
Motion cannot alter both time and space

options.explanation: According to the principles of special relativity, motion can affect both time and space. This is known as time dilation and length contraction.Time dilation: Time passes more slowly for an object that is moving relative to an observer. This effect becomes more pronounced as the object's speed approaches the speed of light.Length contraction: The length of an object moving relative to an observer appears to be shorter in the direction of motion. This effect also becomes more pronounced as the object's speed approaches the speed of light.Therefore, motion can alter both time and space, and the statement (b) is universally correct.

questions.explanation: The correct answer is (b) Motion can alter time and space.According to the principles of special relativity, motion can affect both time and space. This is known as time dilation and length contraction.Time dilation: Time passes more slowly for an object that is moving relative to an observer. This effect becomes more pronounced as the object's speed approaches the speed of light.Length contraction: The length of an object moving relative to an observer appears to be shorter in the direction of motion. This effect also becomes more pronounced as the object's speed approaches the speed of light.Therefore, motion can alter both time and space, and the statement (b) is universally correct.

questions.text: Who proposed matter waves?
questions.subject: Physics

options.explanation: Louis de Broglie, a French physicist, proposed the concept of matter waves in his 1924 PhD thesis. He suggested that all matter, not just light, exhibits wave-like properties. This revolutionary idea was based on the wave-particle duality of light, which had been established by Albert Einstein's work on the photoelectric effect.De Broglie's hypothesis was later confirmed by experiments, most notably by the Davisson-Germer experiment in 1927. This experiment demonstrated the wave-like behavior of electrons.

questions.explanation: Louis de Broglie, a French physicist, proposed the concept of matter waves in his 1924 PhD thesis. He suggested that all matter, not just light, exhibits wave-like properties. This revolutionary idea was based on the wave-particle duality of light, which had been established by Albert Einstein's work on the photoelectric effect.De Broglie's hypothesis was later confirmed by experiments, most notably by the Davisson-Germer experiment in 1927. This experiment demonstrated the wave-like behavior of electrons.

questions.text: Twin paradox is consequence of?
questions.subject: Physics

options.explanation: The twin paradox is a thought experiment in special relativity that explores the consequences of time dilation. It involves two identical twins, one of whom travels at a high speed relative to the other. When the traveling twin returns, they will be younger than the twin who stayed on Earth.This difference in age is a direct result of time dilation, which states that time passes more slowly for an object that is moving relative to an observer. The twin who traveled at high speed experienced time dilation, causing them to age more slowly than the twin who remained on Earth.

questions.explanation: The twin paradox is a thought experiment in special relativity that explores the consequences of time dilation. It involves two identical twins, one of whom travels at a high speed relative to the other. When the traveling twin returns, they will be younger than the twin who stayed on Earth.This difference in age is a direct result of time dilation, which states that time passes more slowly for an object that is moving relative to an observer. The twin who traveled at high speed experienced time dilation, causing them to age more slowly than the twin who remained on Earth.

questions.text: Anti matter theory was proposed by
questions.subject: Physics

options.explanation: Paul Dirac, a British physicist, proposed the existence of antimatter in 1928. He was working on a relativistic equation for the electron, and his solution predicted the existence of a particle with the same mass as the electron but with an opposite charge. This particle was later discovered and named the positron.Dirac's theory revolutionized our understanding of matter and antimatter, and it laid the foundation for the development of particle physics.

questions.explanation: Paul Dirac, a British physicist, proposed the existence of antimatter in 1928. He was working on a relativistic equation for the electron, and his solution predicted the existence of a particle with the same mass as the electron but with an opposite charge. This particle was later discovered and named the positron.Dirac's theory revolutionized our understanding of matter and antimatter, and it laid the foundation for the development of particle physics.

questions.text: The stopping potential depends on
questions.subject: Physics

options.explanation: The stopping potential does depend on both the intensity and frequency of the incident light.Here's why:Frequency: The frequency of the incident light determines the energy of the photons. Higher frequency photons have more energy, which leads to photoelectrons with higher kinetic energy. As a result, a higher stopping potential is required to stop these electrons.Intensity: While the intensity of light doesn't affect the energy of individual photons, it does affect the number of photons hitting the metal surface. Higher intensity means more photons, which in turn means more photoelectrons emitted. This increased current due to more electrons requires a higher stopping potential to completely stop the flow.

Higher frequency photons have more energy, which leads to photoelectrons with higher kinetic energy. As a result, a higher stopping potential is required to stop these electrons.Intensity: While the intensity of light doesn't affect the energy of individual photons, it does affect the number of photons hitting the metal surface. Higher intensity means more photons, which in turn means more photoelectrons emitted. This increased current due to more electrons requires a higher stopping potential to completely stop the flow.

questions.text: Laser light is:
questions.subject: Physics

options.explanation: The full form of laser in literal is Light amplification by stimulated emission of radiation. Lasers produce intense and focused beams of light through a process called stimulated emission. All of these are the features of a laser and without these features a laser would not be formed. These three characteristics differentiate Laser from other light sources and makes it useful.

questions.text: Which series is emitted when electron jumps from a higher orbit to fifth orbit?
questions.subject: Physics

options.explanation: The series emitted is in the order from 1: (5)Lymann → Balmer → Paschen → Brackett → Pfund → HumpherySo, for 5th orbit it is Pfund series.

questions.explanation: The series emitted is in the order from 1: (5)Lymann → Balmer → Paschen → Brackett → Pfund → HumpherySo, for 5th orbit it is Pfund series.

questions.text: Total energy of an electron in hydrogen atom when it jumps to higher orbit from a lower orbit is:
questions.subject: Physics

options.explanation: We know that the total energy of an electron in orbit is negative (due to attraction towards nucleus), given by the relation En = -E0 / n2 We also know that when an electron jumps from lower to higher orbit, it absorbs energy. Moreover, from the equation, we can see that as the value of n increases, the value of En will decrease (inverse relation). Hence, Total energy will be less negative.

questions.explanation: We know that the total energy of an electron in orbit is negative (due to attraction towards nucleus), given by the relation En = -E0 / n2 We also know that when an electron jumps from lower to higher orbit, it absorbs energy. Moreover, from the equation, we can see that as the value of n increases, the value of En will decrease (inverse relation). Hence, Total energy will be less negative.

questions.text: Radiations emitted by human body lie in which of the following series?
questions.subject: Physics

options.explanation: The radiation emitted by the human body is in the form of thermal radiations. This thermal radiation is a continuous spectrum in the infrared region and does not emit the discrete spectral lines of atomic transitions like the series given in the options. The energy levels involved in thermal radiation from the human body are associated with the temperature of the body rather than electronic transitions within atoms. So, the answer is none of these.
